g.data-narration: Data structures organize and store data efficiently, directly impacting algorithm performance in software applications.
g.text: Data structures organize, manage, and store data efficiently. The right choice can make
an app run in milliseconds vs minutes. Essential for efficient algorithm design.

g.data-narration: A data structure is a specialized format for organizing, processing, and storing data for fast operations like search and insert.
Data structures are classified into primitive and non-primitive types, with non-primitive further divided into linear and non-linear structures.
Primitive data types are basic building blocks like integers and floats, while non-primitive types include arrays, linked lists, trees, and graphs.
Linear structures arrange elements sequentially while non-linear structures represent hierarchical relationships between elements.
g.text: Examples: Array, Linked List, Stack, Queue

g.data-narration: Static data structures have fixed sizes determined at compile time, while dynamic structures can grow or shrink during execution.
g.text: Fixed size determined at compile time

g.data-narration: An ADT defines data by its behavior without implementation details, separating interface from how operations actually work.
g.text: Mathematical model defining data by behavior (operations) without implementation details

g.data-narration: Big-O notation describes how execution time grows with input size, from constant O(1) to exponential O(2^n).
g.text: How execution time grows with input size (n)

g.data-narration: This graph shows how different time complexities grow, with O(1) staying flat and exponential rising dramatically.
The six fundamental operations on data structures are traversal, search, insertion, deletion, sorting, and merging.
Arrays provide O(1) random access but O(n) insertion, while linked lists offer O(1) insertion but O(n) access.
A queue follows FIFO order, perfect for print job scheduling where jobs are processed in the order they arrive.
g.text: Example: Queue for Print Job Scheduling
Use Queue (FIFO) - process jobs in arrival order

g.data-narration: For linear search, best case is O(1) when element is first, worst case is O(n) when element is last or absent.
Remember: ADT defines logical form (what operations do), while data structure provides physical implementation (how it works).
g.text: Key Distinction: ADT vs Data Structure

g.data-narration: Study tips: memorize operation complexities, know when to use stack vs queue, and understand Big-O growth rates.
